No Poverty.reason: GIS skills from a Land Economics course directly support land administration, property rights (Target 1.4), and disaster resilience planning (Target 1.5), crucial for poverty reduction.

Zero Hunger.reason: GIS data analysis and mapping skills are directly applicable to sustainable food production, agricultural land management, and monitoring land quality (Target 2.4).

Good Health and Well-being.reason: GIS data analysis and mapping are foundational for spatial epidemiology, identifying pollution sources (Target 3.9), and managing public health risks (Target 3.d).

Quality Education.reason: The course directly provides technical GIS skills for employment (Target 4.4) and fosters knowledge applicable to sustainable development (Target 4.7), aligning with quality education.

Gender Equality.reason: There is no explicit or reasonably inferred connection in the course syllabus to gender equality or any of its specific targets.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: GIS is a fundamental tool for integrated water resources management (Target 6.5) and protecting water-related ecosystems (Target 6.6) through spatial data analysis.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: GIS data analysis skills are widely used for planning energy infrastructure and optimal siting of renewable energy projects (Targets 7.a, 7.b).

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course provides technical GIS skills for employment, contributing to productive work (Target 8.5) and economic productivity (Target 8.2) through technological application.

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: GIS is a fundamental technology for planning, developing, and managing quality, reliable, sustainable, and resilient infrastructure (Target 9.1), and enhances scientific research (Target 9.5).

Reduced Inequalities.reason: GIS data analysis and mapping skills can be used to identify and analyze spatial inequalities in access to services and resources (Target 10.2).

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: GIS is a core tool for sustainable urbanization, human settlement planning (Target 11.3), and building resilience to disasters (Target 11.b), central to Land Economics.

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: GIS skills in spatial monitoring and data analysis support the sustainable management and efficient use of natural resources (Target 12.2).

Climate Action.reason: GIS is critical for strengthening resilience and adaptive capacity to climate-related hazards (Target 13.1) through spatial data analysis and risk mapping.

Life Below Water.reason: GIS skills in mapping and analysis are applicable to managing marine and coastal ecosystems (Target 14.2) and conserving marine areas (Target 14.5).

Life on Land.reason: GIS is fundamental for managing terrestrial ecosystems (Target 15.1), combating land degradation (Target 15.3), and integrating biodiversity into planning (Target 15.9), core to Land Economics.

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: GIS can enhance transparency and accountability in land administration (Target 16.6), contributing to stronger institutions, especially relevant for Land Economics.

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The course teaches skills for managing geographically located data (Target 17.18), which is essential for monitoring and reporting progress across all SDGs.
